question: How does a tokamak confine fusion plasma?

options: A toroidal magnetic field (from external coils) plus a poloidal field (from a large plasma current driven through the gas) twist into helical field lines on nested flux surfaces, confining the hot plasma; the combined field prevents the charged particles' cross-field drifts from rapidly losing them to the walls
It uses gravity to hold the plasma together (this reverses the actual relationship) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this conflates cause and effect)
It confines plasma with electrostatic fields only (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
It relies on a solid container at fusion temperatures (true only as a special case, not in general) (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: No material can contain a 100-million-kelvin plasma, so magnetic confinement is used. In a tokamak (toroidal chamber with magnetic coils), strong toroidal-field coils produce the main field; a transformer (or other current drive) induces a toroidal plasma current that creates a poloidal field. The two combine into helical field lines wrapping nested toroidal flux surfaces. This twist is essential: a purely toroidal field would let grad-B and curvature drifts separate charges and drive the plasma out, but the rotational transform (field-line twist, measured by the safety factor $q$) short-circuits this drift, giving confinement. Tokamaks are the leading magnetic-confinement concept; ITER (under construction) aims for $Q=10$ (ten times more fusion power out than heating power in). Challenges include current drive for steady state, disruptions, and exhaust handling at the divertor.

active.prompt: Match each fusion-confinement concept with its meaning:

pairs.definition: Toroidal device; external toroidal field + induced plasma-current poloidal field; needs current drive
3D twisted external coils provide the full twist; intrinsically steady-state, no plasma current needed
Field-line twist (toroidal/poloidal turns); $q$-profile controls MHD stability (kinks at low $q$)
$nT\tau_E$ measures progress to ignition; $Q$ = fusion power / heating power

active.explanation: Tokamaks and stellarators are the two main toroidal confinement concepts. Tokamaks achieve the best confinement but rely on a plasma current (risking disruptions and complicating steady-state operation); stellarators put all the field-line twist in elaborate 3D coils, gaining intrinsic steady-state stability at the cost of engineering complexity (Wendelstein 7-X is the flagship). Both target the Lawson triple product.

question: Why is deuterium-tritium the favoured fusion fuel?

options: D + T $\to\,^4$He (3.5 MeV) + n (14.1 MeV) has the largest cross-section at the lowest temperature (peak near 65 keV) of accessible fusion reactions; the alpha stays to heat the plasma, the neutron carries energy out to breed tritium and generate heat
Because D and T are the heaviest available fuels (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Because it produces no neutrons (this conflates cause and effect) (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence)
Because it requires the highest ignition temperature (true only as a special case, not in general) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: Among candidate fusion reactions, deuterium-tritium has by far the highest reactivity at the lowest temperature, with a cross-section peaking near 65 keV (~$5\times$ easier than D-D), making it the realistic first-generation fuel. It releases 17.6 MeV per reaction: the 3.5 MeV alpha particle is charged and remains magnetically confined to heat the plasma (enabling self-sustaining 'burning plasma'), while the 14.1 MeV neutron escapes to deposit energy in a surrounding blanket — which also breeds tritium via $^6$Li + n $\to$ T + $^4$He (essential, since tritium is radioactive with a 12.3-yr half-life and not naturally abundant). Drawbacks: the energetic neutrons activate and damage structural materials and require shielding/remote handling. Advanced 'aneutronic' fuels (D-$^3$He, p-$^{11}$B) avoid most neutrons but demand far higher temperatures and have lower reactivity, so D-T is the route for ITER and near-term reactors.

question: What is the difference between breakeven, ignition, and the fusion gain Q?

options: $Q$ = fusion power / external heating power; scientific breakeven is $Q=1$; ignition is $Q\to\infty$ (alpha self-heating sustains the burn without external heating); reactors need $Q\gtrsim10$ (ITER's goal) for net electricity after plant inefficiencies
They all mean exactly the same thing (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Ignition means the plasma cools down (this conflates cause and effect) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a common misconception)
Breakeven requires Q below 1 (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: The fusion energy gain factor $Q = P_{\rm fusion}/P_{\rm heating}$ benchmarks performance. Scientific breakeven is $Q=1$ (fusion power equals the external heating power injected into the plasma). Ignition is the limit $Q\to\infty$: the confined alpha particles deposit enough energy to sustain the plasma temperature with no external heating — a self-sustaining burn (achieved transiently at NIF in 2022 in the inertial sense). Because converting heat to electricity is ~30-40% efficient and the plant has recirculating power needs, a power-producing reactor requires engineering breakeven and practically $Q\gtrsim10$ (ITER's target) up to $\sim$30-50 for an economical plant (DEMO). Note distinctions: 'scientific' vs 'engineering' breakeven, and gain referenced to power into the plasma vs into the whole facility (NIF's headline gain was capsule-referenced, far above the wall-plug). These definitions are essential for honestly assessing fusion progress.

question: What is the H-mode and why does it matter?

options: A high-confinement regime, accessed above a heating-power threshold, in which a sheared-flow edge transport barrier (steep pedestal) suppresses turbulence and roughly doubles the energy confinement time versus the L-mode; it is the baseline scenario for ITER, but its pedestal drives periodic ELMs
A low-confinement mode that wastes energy (this describes a different phenomenon) (a common misconception) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
A mode requiring no heating at all (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (not supported by the evidence)
An instability that destroys the plasma instantly (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Discovered on ASDEX (1982), the high-confinement mode (H-mode) appears spontaneously when auxiliary heating exceeds a threshold: a region of strong sheared $\mathbf E\times\mathbf B$ flow forms at the plasma edge, tearing apart turbulent eddies and creating an edge transport barrier — a steep pressure 'pedestal' that lifts the whole temperature/density profile and roughly doubles the confinement time relative to the low-confinement L-mode. This jump is crucial: ITER's performance projections assume H-mode. The catch is the steep pedestal becomes unstable to edge-localised modes (ELMs) — quasi-periodic relaxations that expel bursts of energy and particles onto the divertor, tolerable in present machines but potentially damaging at ITER/reactor scale. Hence intense research on ELM control (resonant magnetic perturbations, pellet pacing) and naturally ELM-free regimes (QH-mode, I-mode, negative-triangularity). H-mode physics — turbulence suppression by sheared flow — is a centerpiece of confinement science.

question: What are the main milestones and challenges on the path to fusion power?

options: Milestones: JET D-T records, NIF ignition (2022), ITER (burning plasma, $Q=10$); challenges include steady-state operation, plasma-facing materials and neutron damage, tritium breeding/self-sufficiency, divertor heat exhaust, disruptions, and overall plant economics
Fusion power has been on the grid for decades (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
There are no remaining technical challenges (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect)
The only challenge is finding deuterium (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: Scientific progress is real but engineering hurdles remain. Milestones: tokamaks raised the triple product over decades; JET set magnetic-fusion energy records (59 MJ in 2021 D-T); NIF achieved inertial ignition (2022); ITER (international, under construction) aims to demonstrate a burning plasma with $Q=10$ and the technologies for a reactor. The hard challenges: achieving steady-state (non-inductive current drive or stellarator geometry); plasma-facing materials and structures that survive intense 14-MeV neutron fluence (activation, swelling, embrittlement) and enormous divertor heat loads (tens of MW/m$^2$); breeding enough tritium in lithium blankets to be fuel self-sufficient (tritium breeding ratio > 1); avoiding/mitigating disruptions; and ultimately economics and reliability for a power plant (DEMO and beyond). Private ventures pursue high-field (HTS-magnet) compact tokamaks and alternative concepts to accelerate timelines. Fusion's promise — abundant fuel, no carbon, no long-lived high-level waste, inherent safety — keeps it a major energy-research goal despite the remaining road.

question: How do high-temperature superconducting magnets change the fusion outlook?

options: REBCO high-temperature superconductors sustain much higher magnetic fields than conventional Nb-based magnets; since fusion power density scales steeply with field (roughly $B^4$ at fixed shape), stronger fields enable far more compact, potentially cheaper, faster-to-build tokamaks (e.g. SPARC/ARC) at higher $Q$
They make magnets weaker and devices larger (this reverses the actual relationship) (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Superconductors are irrelevant to fusion (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence)
Higher field reduces fusion power density (true only as a special case, not in general) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Confinement and fusion performance improve sharply with magnetic field: at fixed plasma shape and beta, fusion power density scales roughly as $B^4$ and the achievable triple product/$Q$ rise steeply, so a higher field lets you shrink the machine dramatically (volume, and hence cost and build time, falls). Conventional low-temperature superconductors (NbTi, Nb$_3$Sn, used in ITER) are limited to ~12 T at the coil. New high-temperature superconductors — especially REBCO (rare-earth barium copper oxide) coated tapes — carry high current density at much higher fields (>20 T) and warmer, more forgiving temperatures, enabling compact high-field tokamaks. This underpins ventures like Commonwealth Fusion Systems' SPARC (targeting $Q>1$) and the ARC reactor concept, and MIT's 2021 demonstration of a 20-T REBCO toroidal-field coil. By trading machine size for magnet technology, HTS magnets are widely seen as a potential game-changer accelerating the timeline to demonstration fusion power, though materials, quench protection, and neutron tolerance of the magnets remain active engineering challenges.
